title: Girlfriends and Boyfriends

jokes.text: Nick deflects the group's teasing about Lindsay with 'I don't kiss and tell' — delivered with practiced cool while clearly delighted
jokes.setup: The freaks immediately clock that Nick and Lindsay are together and demand to know if they're 'going out'
jokes.punchline: Nick plays it cool with a cliché that simultaneously confirms everything
jokes.explanation: Works because Nick is performing coolness to his friends while Lindsay is right there. The phrase is a cliché but Nick deploying it with earnest pride is character-specific and gets a mild laugh.

jokes.text: One of the freaks yells 'Grab me a piece!' at Nick and Lindsay, who are clearly just hanging out
jokes.setup: Nick has just smoothly handled the group's questions about Lindsay
jokes.punchline: An unseen freak immediately blows up Nick's cool act with a crude shout
jokes.explanation: Quick, functional joke that deflates Nick's momentary suavity. The embarrassment Nick shows ('I'm sorry about that') is the real joke — his friends undercut him immediately.

jokes.text: Millie's declaration: 'Freaks only date freak girls. And you're not a freak girl.' / 'How do you know?' / ''Cause freaks go all the way.'
jokes.setup: Millie confronts Lindsay about kissing Nick and warns her off the relationship
jokes.punchline: Millie's ironclad freaks-go-all-the-way logic delivered with complete sincerity by the most straight-laced girl in school
jokes.explanation: This is excellent character comedy. The joke only works because it's Millie — the church girl delivering a pseudo-sociological taxonomy of high school sexuality as though it's settled fact. The escalation from 'freaks only date freak girls' to 'freaks go all the way' is perfectly structured and completely Millie. Her earnest certainty makes it funnier than any self-aware joke could be. Earns its 7.8 craft score.

jokes.text: Millie: 'What about Patty Filker? She's a freak girl. And she has a baby! But I guess that's no big deal either.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay dismisses Millie's freak-girl theory
jokes.punchline: Millie produces a case study — Patty Filker, freak girl, has a baby — as definitive evidence
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'freaks go all the way' to producing a named peer with a baby as proof is perfectly executed. 'But I guess that's no big deal either' is a devastating passive-aggressive closer. Millie's righteous smugness is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: Neal says he doesn't want to be late for bio because 'Yesterday I was late, and everyone looked at me.'
jokes.setup: Sam rushes Neal to class
jokes.punchline: Neal's reason for urgency is not academic concern but a paralyzing self-consciousness about being looked at
jokes.explanation: Compact and true to Neal's anxious, self-important character. The joke lands because the fear of being looked at is relatable but his deadpan delivery of it as a serious concern is funny.

jokes.text: Sam gets a locker slam right after saying hi to Cindy, and someone mocks him: 'Ho ho, hey, Merv Griffin! Nice interview.'
jokes.setup: Sam manages to squeak out a 'Hi, Cindy' with a brief exchange
jokes.punchline: Immediately mocked for the lameness of his interaction — the 'Merv Griffin' comparison reducing his attempt at suave conversation to a talk show segment
jokes.explanation: Period-specific insult (Merv Griffin as a synonym for stiff, formal small talk) that works as both a laugh and as worldbuilding cruelty. Sam's impotent cringe in response is half the joke.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso: 'I got it on in a van at Woodstock, I'm not judging anybody.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay says she and Nick are 'just friends' and Rosso doesn't need to worry
jokes.punchline: Rosso overshares his Woodstock sexual history as a credential for non-judgment
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. Mr. Rosso is defined by the gap between his role as guidance counselor and his total inability to maintain professional boundaries — this line is the platonic ideal of that character. 'I got it on in a van at Woodstock' is specific, visual, and immediately hilarious. Delivered with the confidence of someone who thinks this makes him relatable. Earns a high craft score because it could only come from this character and is perfectly economical.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso describes meeting a woman at 'the discotheque at the bowling alley on 15 Mile' as the prelude to his herpes story
jokes.setup: Rosso is trying to warn Lindsay about emotional confusion and STDs
jokes.punchline: His cautionary anecdote starts at a bowling alley discotheque — the specific, deeply unglamorous detail does more work than any punchline could
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'the discotheque at the bowling alley on 15 Mile' is a masterclass in comedy writing. It's so precise and so sad — exactly the kind of detail a guidance counselor in Michigan would produce. The mundane reality undercuts any attempt at gravitas.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso, after a long pause: 'I have herpes.'
jokes.setup: A long buildup about a woman at a bowling alley discotheque, a kiss, 'one thing led to another'
jokes.punchline: The blunt, matter-of-fact declaration 'I have herpes' delivered by a guidance counselor to a student he's trying to counsel
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. Three words. Maximum impact. The structure is perfect: the buildup is long enough to make you forget the punchline is coming, and then 'I have herpes' lands like a bomb. The pause before it (implied by the timestamp gap and the 'And...' structure) is crucial. It's the economy that makes it exceptional — Rosso doesn't add anything, doesn't soften it, doesn't explain. It earns comparison to top-tier sitcom economy. The fact that he's a guidance counselor making this confession to a 16-year-old student makes it dark-subversive as well.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso following up: 'You don't want it!' — after Lindsay is visibly horrified
jokes.setup: Lindsay is clearly desperate to leave after the herpes revelation
jokes.punchline: Rosso's panicked clarification ('You don't want it!') as if Lindsay might have missed that herpes is bad
jokes.explanation: The follow-up lands because Rosso's earnestness is so complete — he thinks she might need to be told that herpes is undesirable. The callback squeeze out a second laugh from a joke that already peaked.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso: 'I just blew your mind, didn't I?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is clearly trying to escape the office
jokes.punchline: Rosso interprets her horror as intellectual awakening and seems genuinely proud
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat. Rosso's self-satisfaction — that he's done meaningful guidance work by disclosing his STI — is the heart of his character. The line is quotable and captures his obliviousness completely.

jokes.text: Sam: 'Maybe he takes baths instead. My mom says that they don't really get you clean.' / Neal: 'Yeah, you just sit in your own filth.'
jokes.setup: Neal wonders if Gordon ever showers
jokes.punchline: Sam's charitable bath hypothesis, immediately demolished by the 'own filth' observation, delivered as though this is scientific fact
jokes.explanation: Good double-act exchange. Sam's politeness (defending Gordon) and Neal's pragmatism ('your own filth') is a small but clean comedy beat. The 'my mom says' addition is a nice character touch for Sam.

jokes.text: Teacher: 'We're going to be dissecting kittens.' [students gasp] 'Just trying to get your attention.'
jokes.setup: Teacher tries to get the class settled
jokes.punchline: Kitten dissection as an attention-grabbing fake-out, delivered flatly
jokes.explanation: A classic fake-out that works because of the gasps it earns and the teacher's complete indifference. Slightly generic but executed well. The teacher barely registers as a character so the character integration is low.

jokes.text: Bill is assigned as Cindy's lab partner — Sam's crushed reaction as Bill smugly notes 'Me and Cindy, you and Gordon'
jokes.setup: Sam desperately hopes to be paired with Cindy; we know Gordon reeks
jokes.punchline: Sam gets Gordon, Bill gets Cindy — the worst possible outcome delivered with Bill's complete lack of awareness of what this means to Sam
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured comedic misfortune. Sam loses Cindy to his own friend AND gets paired with the smelly kid. The show earns the laugh through setup (we know both Gordon's smell and Sam's crush) rather than through a clever line.

jokes.text: Sam mutters: 'Well, there goes Cindy's grade point average.'
jokes.setup: Bill has just been assigned as Cindy's lab partner
jokes.punchline: Sam's immediate pivot to sour-grapes academic commentary
jokes.explanation: Good sour-grapes line. Sam immediately writing off Bill as a lab partner for Cindy is petty and teenage in a funny way. Economy is excellent — one line does double duty (reveals Sam's jealousy AND insults Bill).

jokes.text: Teacher's line 'Science waits...' and Gordon completes it with 'for no man' — suggesting Gordon is an unlikely science nerd
jokes.setup: The teacher starts a familiar-sounding phrase
jokes.punchline: Gordon enthusiastically completes it — this smelly, ostracized kid is a genuine science devotee, subverting expectations
jokes.explanation: Small character beat that deepens Gordon — his enthusiastic participation hints at his hidden earnestness. Not a big laugh but adds texture.

jokes.text: Daniel keeps telling Lindsay how great Nick is — three separate times — with escalating desperation: 'He's a really great guy... He's the man... Nick's a stud, you know? I mean, he may not seem like it, but he is.'
jokes.setup: Daniel wants Lindsay to understand Nick is great
jokes.punchline: The escalating repetition — 'he may not seem like it, but he is' — inadvertently suggests Nick is exactly not a stud
jokes.explanation: The escalating repetition is structurally smart: Daniel's endorsement becomes self-undermining ('he may not seem like it') and his whispering urgency makes it funnier. It reveals Daniel's genuine loyalty to Nick while being hilariously ineffective as a sales pitch. The running-gag structure across the scene earns multiple laughs.

jokes.text: Daniel whispering conspiratorially: 'Nick's a stud... you know? I mean, he may not seem like it, but he is.'
jokes.setup: Daniel has been insisting Nick is great; now he lowers his voice to whisper this final credential
jokes.punchline: 'He may not seem like it, but he is' — the qualification that negates the compliment
jokes.explanation: The peak of Daniel's awkward bro-advocacy. The whisper adds a layer of absurdity — what's the point of whispering 'Nick's a stud' — and the self-defeating qualifier is the episode's best accidental-undercut joke. Perfectly character-integrated: only Daniel, who is too cool to show open vulnerability, would lobby for his friend this obliquely.

jokes.text: Teacher to Daniel: 'Mr. Dizario, don't you have something to do?' / Daniel: 'Right, but I thought we were going to do that after class, Miss Yeats.' / Teacher: 'Ha ha. Be still, my beating heart.'
jokes.setup: Miss Yeats calls Daniel out for disrupting class
jokes.punchline: Daniel flirts with her; she delivers a perfectly withering 'Be still, my beating heart' without missing a beat
jokes.explanation: Efficient two-liner. Daniel's brazen flirt (propositioning a teacher mid-class) and the teacher's dry, expressionless rejection is a good dynamic. 'Be still, my beating heart' is a period-appropriate and character-specific putdown.

jokes.text: Sam can't eat his tuna salad because 'It smells like Gordon.'
jokes.setup: Gordon's smell has been established; Sam sits at lunch
jokes.punchline: The smell has colonized Sam's perception of his own food
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Gordon's smell. Brief, economical, and extends the running gag. Tuna salad is a particularly good choice given its pre-existing reputation.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Yeah, Cindy smells, too. Like flowers.' [laughs to himself]
jokes.setup: Sam says the tuna smells like Gordon
jokes.punchline: Neal casually redirects to Cindy's smell — flowers — and seems deeply pleased with himself about it
jokes.explanation: Neal's non-sequitur redirect to Cindy's smell (and his satisfaction with the observation) is a small but effective character moment. His laugh at his own observation is classic Neal.

jokes.text: Neal: 'I don't know. Her tongue was in my ear the whole time.' [re: Cindy's thoughts about Sam]
jokes.setup: Sam desperately asks if Cindy said anything about him while she and Neal were lab partners
jokes.punchline: Neal claims Cindy had her tongue in his ear — a complete fabrication delivered with casual confidence to wind Sam up
jokes.explanation: Excellent Neal moment. The image is ridiculous — Cindy Sanders doesn't have her tongue in anyone's ear — but Neal delivers it with pure deadpan confidence. Classic middle-school braggadocio, perfectly timed to maximum torment Sam.

jokes.text: Cindy says 'Hi, Sam. Hey, Nate.' / Neal: 'It's Neal—' [cut off immediately by Cindy moving on]
jokes.setup: Cindy approaches the table
jokes.punchline: She gets Neal's name wrong, and he barely gets a syllable of correction out before she's moved on
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. Neal's truncated correction ('It's Neal—') cut off mid-syllable is devastatingly executed. It's a tiny joke with real punch: Cindy doesn't even know his name, and he doesn't rate enough to finish his objection. The show's naturalism makes this land harder than it would in a broader comedy.

jokes.text: Bill, after Cindy invites him to her house at 4:30: 'I think—I think I could squeeze you in.'
jokes.setup: Cindy asks if Bill can come over to study
jokes.punchline: Bill — a nerdy kid with no discernible social calendar — gravely considers his schedule and decides he can 'squeeze her in'
jokes.explanation: Bill's unearned suavity is perfectly calibrated. The pause before 'I think I could squeeze you in' suggests he's genuinely consulting an imaginary packed schedule. Only Bill — earnest, uncool, completely unaware of his own nerdiness — could deliver this with a straight face. One of the episode's better character comedy moments.

jokes.text: Sam asks his friends: 'Should I wear Hai Karate or Old Spice?'
jokes.setup: Sam is excited that Bill gets to go to Cindy's house — and by extension, Sam might get to see Cindy
jokes.punchline: Sam's strategic cologne question; his friends treat it with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: Period-specific detail (Hai Karate!) that doubles as character comedy — Sam treating a science assignment as a romantic opportunity. Neal's 'Definitely Old Spice' adds to the joke by taking the question completely seriously.

jokes.text: Kim defends donkey basketball's ethics: 'Oh, yeah, like the donkeys really give a care.' / Lindsay: 'Well, how would you like some big fat teacher sitting on your back while you had to run around a gym?'
jokes.setup: Someone mentions donkey basketball is coming up
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's weirdly specific donkey-sympathizer argument vs. Kim's breezy dismissal of donkey interiority
jokes.explanation: A small exchange that's funnier than it looks on paper. 'The donkeys really give a care' is a good Kim-ism, and Lindsay's earnest advocacy for the donkeys' dignity is characteristically Lindsay. Brief but works.

jokes.text: Teacher (hall monitor): 'Lord and Lady Skips-A-Lot, come on, let's go!' and 'Last one in class, first one on welfare. It's your choice.'
jokes.setup: The bell rings and students dawdle
jokes.punchline: Two rapid-fire lines — a mock-royal title and a welfare threat — from a teacher who has clearly said these too many times
jokes.explanation: 'Lord and Lady Skips-A-Lot' is a genuinely good line — creative, specific to the era, and immediately funny. The welfare line is darker and more memorable. Both work because they feel like real teacher weapons honed over years.

jokes.text: Nick responds to the teacher's command with 'Yes, ma'am—I mean, sir.' [hall erupts]
jokes.setup: The teacher (gender ambiguous or female presenting as male-coded in Nick's perception) is shooing students to class
jokes.punchline: Nick's accidental ma'am-to-sir correction makes everything worse
jokes.explanation: Classic awkward-correction joke. The laughter from the crowd and Nick's flustered 'I was actually—' make it land. Slightly generic but serves the scene.

jokes.text: Bill performs an Obi-Wan impression from Star Wars sheets in Sam's room: '[imitates Obi-Wan] May the Force be with you.'
jokes.setup: Bill and Cindy are at Sam's house studying; Cindy admires Sam's Star Wars sheets
jokes.punchline: Bill immediately does an Obi-Wan impression — the sheets are so embarrassingly childish that Bill's instinct is to perform
jokes.explanation: The Star Wars sheets are a perfect visual gag — Sam's childhood bedroom is adorably embarrassing in front of Cindy. Bill's Obi-Wan impression adds a second layer: even Bill thinks they're funny. Cindy's polite 'Oh, wow. Cool sheets!' is the straight-man response that makes it work.

jokes.text: Bill does the Horshack laugh from Welcome Back Kotter at Cindy, completely unprompted, and then has to explain it.
jokes.setup: Bill offers to watch Welcome Back Kotter
jokes.punchline: He launches into the Horshack laugh, Cindy stares blankly, and he has to explain 'That's Horshack... from the show'
jokes.explanation: Beautiful cringe moment. Bill's social obliviousness — launching a character impression at someone who has no context for it — is pure Bill. The explanation ('That's Horshack... from the show') is the kind of unnecessary clarification that makes the hole deeper. The beat between the laugh and Cindy's 'Are you OK?' is the real punchline.

jokes.text: Cindy: 'I don't really watch TV. Except for the Muppet Show.' / Bill: '[reacts in shock]' / '[long pause]'
jokes.setup: Bill has just done a Horshack impression for someone who doesn't watch TV
jokes.punchline: Cindy's admission that she doesn't watch TV lands as a fundamental incompatibility — Bill's entire personality is TV-based — and his visible shock
jokes.explanation: Bill's worldview is constructed entirely from television. Someone saying they don't watch TV is an existential disruption. The Muppet Show exception (only Muppets!) makes it funnier. The pause after her confession is the awkward silence that is itself the joke.

jokes.text: Cindy stands up from a chair and it makes a fart-like noise. She immediately says: 'This stupid chair always does that.'
jokes.setup: Bill and Cindy are having an awkward study session
jokes.punchline: The chair makes a flatulent sound at a crucial moment of attempted social normalcy; Cindy's instant defensive attribution is both believable and suspicious
jokes.explanation: Setup for a running gag that pays off brilliantly later. On its own it's a decent cringe moment — Cindy is mortified, Bill's reaction face presumably carries it — but in context of the later 'she cut the cheese' controversy, this moment becomes a foundational joke that earns a rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Long silent beat (15:12–15:32) after Cindy leaves to get lemonade — Bill alone with the chair, clearly processing what just happened
jokes.setup: Cindy has departed the scene of the chair incident
jokes.punchline: The extended silence as Bill sits with what just happened — possibly looking at the chair
jokes.explanation: Single-camera naturalist comedy often lives in these silences. Whether Bill is mortified for Cindy, genuinely uncertain, or quietly pleased that she seems human — the beat is funny in the watching, less so in the transcription.

jokes.text: Neal's advice to Sam: 'Find out her schedule and memorize it. That way, you can bump into her and say hi.'
jokes.setup: Sam wants to know how to get closer to Cindy
jokes.punchline: Neal's surveillance-based courtship strategy is delivered as standard romantic advice, unaware that 'memorize her schedule' sounds genuinely stalker-ish
jokes.explanation: Neal's romantic advice is perfectly character-integrated: he's the kid who thinks he has adult knowledge but is actually just generating protocols. The escalating advice plan (schedule, clubs, hair compliments) is funny as a package.

jokes.text: Neal's advice escalates to: 'Join all her after-school clubs... And C — very important — you can make sure she's not falling in love with anyone else, like your friend Bill.'
jokes.setup: Neal's courtship strategy keeps expanding
jokes.punchline: The reveal that 'C' in the plan is surveillance of Bill — the threat Neal has identified — is simultaneously ridiculous and, in context, prescient
jokes.explanation: The joke earns a rewatch bonus because Neal's paranoia about Bill is actually the correct read on the situation. The escalation from 'join her clubs' to 'monitor your friend' is funny and slightly dark.

jokes.text: Kim jokes: 'I heard Kim got an A on her World Civ test. That's great. Oh no, that's right — that was my girlfriend.' / then: 'Lindsay got detention for flipping off her gym teacher. Oh no, that's right — that was your girlfriend.'
jokes.setup: Kim and Daniel are in a running joke about confusing their girlfriends' achievements
jokes.punchline: Kim repeatedly deploys the 'oh no, that was MY girlfriend'/'YOUR girlfriend' switcheroo to mock both Daniel's new interest in Lindsay and Kim's own status
jokes.explanation: Kim's sarcastic 'girlfriend confusion' bit is a good showcase for her character — she's clearly bothered by Daniel and Lindsay's closeness but expresses it through mockery rather than vulnerability. The 'flipping off her gym teacher' detail is a nice character beat for Lindsay.

jokes.text: Kim: 'I heard Kim punched you in the chest really hard. Oh, whoops. That was me!' — and then punches Daniel
jokes.setup: Kim's running switcheroo joke
jokes.punchline: The joke becomes physical as Kim literally punches Daniel as its punchline
jokes.explanation: The escalation from verbal to physical is perfectly executed. Kim announcing what she's about to do ('Oh, whoops. That was me!') and then doing it is a great comedic structure. Daniel's 'That was really hard' afterward is the needed button. Kim is established as genuinely dangerous in a funny way. The economy — three words before the punch — is excellent.

jokes.text: Daniel: 'I'm telling you, she's abnormal. She eats carrot sticks and she likes Miss Piggy.'
jokes.setup: Daniel (re: Bill at Cindy's) relays that Cindy doesn't eat junk food and watches The Muppet Show
jokes.punchline: Daniel's taxonomy of abnormality — carrot sticks + Miss Piggy = freak — delivered with genuine concern
jokes.explanation: The pairing of 'carrot sticks' and 'Miss Piggy' as co-equal markers of weirdness is absurdist and funny. Daniel treating these as diagnostic criteria is very Daniel.

jokes.text: Sam: 'Well, some chairs make weird noises!' / Neal: 'What kind of a chair was it?' / Sam: 'I don't know. Vinyl?' / Neal: 'Well, vinyl chairs always squeak.' / Sam: 'It wasn't a squeak. It was the sound of cheese being cut.'
jokes.setup: Sam desperately defends Cindy's honor; the chair becomes the central point of forensic debate
jokes.punchline: Sam inadvertently corroborates Neal's claim with 'It wasn't a squeak. It was the sound of cheese being cut.' — he's now arguing AGAINST the chair theory
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic highlight alongside the Rosso herpes confession. Sam's self-defeating defense — accidentally providing expert acoustic testimony AGAINST the chair defense — is structurally perfect. The escalation through the vinyl chair debate ('What kind of chair was it?') to Sam's inadvertent confession is beautifully constructed. 'It was the sound of cheese being cut' is a line that would not be improved by a single word change. It scores comparably to an 8-level anchor joke in structure and character integration — only the subject matter (a fart) keeps it from being called prestige TV writing, but in a show about 14-year-olds, this IS prestige writing.

jokes.text: Sam accuses Bill: 'I think you're making all of this up because you want me to stop liking her because you like her now.'
jokes.setup: The fart debate has escalated to full paranoia
jokes.punchline: Sam's conspiracy theory — Bill invented the fart story because he's in love with Cindy — is both wrong and understandable
jokes.explanation: Sam's jealousy logic is hilariously misplaced — the real enemy is the chair — and his indignation here is very teenage. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Sam's hair compliment to Cindy: 'You changed your hair. It just looks really flat today.'
jokes.setup: Neal advised Sam to compliment Cindy's hair as a way to manufacture conversation; Sam tries this immediately
jokes.punchline: Sam's version of a hair compliment is 'It just looks really flat today' — a flat-out insult delivered as appreciation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The payoff to Neal's hair-compliment advice strategy is catastrophic: Sam has memorized the advice ('did you change your hair?') but has no social intelligence to execute it, resulting in 'it just looks really flat today.' The callback structure is perfect, the character integration is total (only Sam could do this), and the economy is flawless. Earns its 8.2 craft score — it's comparable to the 8.0 anchor in structure and character specificity.

jokes.text: Sam realizes Bill has won Cindy over through Welcome Back Kotter. Bill: 'Yeah, I'm telling ya, that guy is totally underrated.'
jokes.setup: Cindy has bonded with Bill over Welcome Back Kotter — the show she previously said she'd never seen
jokes.punchline: Bill, who couldn't sell himself at all as a person, has successfully sold Cindy on Mr. Woodman from a 70s sitcom, and is very smug about it
jokes.explanation: The reversal on 'Cindy doesn't watch TV' paying off via Kotter is well-constructed. Bill's Woodman evangelism ('totally underrated') delivered with complete authority is classic Bill. The scene cuts on his smug laugh, letting the joke breathe.

jokes.text: Teacher to Lindsay: 'Lindsay, it's a test, not a race. And if it were a race, Millie already beat you.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay finishes a test quickly or is staring into space
jokes.punchline: The Millie comparison as an academic put-down — delivered by a teacher who clearly knows Millie is the gold standard
jokes.explanation: Good line. 'If it were a race, Millie already beat you' is specific and slightly devastating — Millie as the yardstick of academic virtue. The teacher knowing the Millie hierarchy adds texture.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Millie's shared memory: They used to kiss pictures of Leif Garrett from Dynamite magazine, then went to see him at the State Fair and it turned out to be Jack Albertson from Chico and the Man.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is trying to reconnect with Millie by invoking shared teenage girl history
jokes.punchline: The Leif Garrett pilgrimage ending in Jack Albertson is a perfectly specific suburban disappointment
jokes.explanation: Beautifully specific cultural joke. The bait-and-switch of expecting teen idol Leif Garrett and getting 73-year-old Jack Albertson from Chico and the Man is a very precise comedy of era and disappointment. Only this show, set exactly in 1980 Michigan, would produce this joke. Millie's 'It wasn't so bad. It was kinda funny' is a perfect character response — she made peace with it.

jokes.text: Millie: 'Once you do [have sex], you'll never be the same again. Plus no one would want to marry you. Why should they buy the cow when they can get the milk for free?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay says she thinks Nick wants to have sex
jokes.punchline: Millie's unsolicited sexual economics lecture ('buy the cow / milk for free') delivered with complete Christian-girl certainty to her increasingly horrified former friend
jokes.explanation: The cow metaphor is a cultural cliché but Millie deploying it with utter conviction — as though it's a natural law — is character-perfect. The escalation (never the same / no one marries you / cow) works because each step feels inevitable in Millie's worldview.

jokes.text: Teacher Mainzer after a student's dark diary entry: 'OK. Thank you, Sylvia Plath. It's a little dark. We want to sell yearbooks, not tell the truth.'
jokes.setup: A student reads a bleak stream-of-consciousness diary entry about freshman isolation
jokes.punchline: 'We want to sell yearbooks, not tell the truth' — a perfectly cynical teacher line that's also accidentally profound
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. 'We want to sell yearbooks, not tell the truth' does double work: it's funny as a teacher bureaucratically crushing a student's honest writing, AND it's a wry comment on the show's own project (telling the truth about high school). The Sylvia Plath reference is earned because the diary writing IS that overwrought. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Mrs. Weir excitedly shows Lindsay the card with the rose, interrogates her about her 'boyfriend'
jokes.setup: Lindsay comes home to find her mom has intercepted a gift from Nick
jokes.punchline: The maternal excitement is completely disproportionate — 'I want to know everything, really' — rendering Lindsay's private romantic life a spectator sport
jokes.explanation: Reliable parent-embarrassment cringe. Not a strong joke independently but functions well as scene setup.

jokes.text: Sam's dad reacts to seeing Cindy Sanders: 'Quite a cute little number.'
jokes.setup: Sam mentions having a yearbook partner at the store
jokes.punchline: Mr. Weir's response is the uncouth, era-appropriate 'cute little number' — humiliating for Sam, mortifying in retrospect
jokes.explanation: Very Mr. Weir — the dad who says the embarrassing thing without any awareness. 'Little number' is exactly 1980 dad vernacular. Lindsay's 'God, Dad!' is the necessary reaction.

jokes.text: Mr. Weir: '1/8 for $25?! Sam, that's highway robbery! I can get a full page in a Sunday paper for a hundred.'
jokes.setup: Sam pitches the yearbook ad pricing
jokes.punchline: Mr. Weir's immediate market analysis — aggressively applying adult business logic to a kid's yearbook fundraiser — and then immediately buying a full page anyway
jokes.explanation: Mr. Weir's complaint followed immediately by a full-page purchase is a good small joke: he negotiates against himself, can't stop being a dad. Character-integrated because only a Weir-type dad would lecture about highway robbery and then fold immediately for his kid.

jokes.text: Mr. Weir, having urged Sam and Cindy to split up for efficiency, immediately reverses: 'But then again, some of the greatest salesmen worked in teams.' Then reverses AGAIN: 'Yeah, but we'll both finish sooner if we split up, don't you think?'
jokes.setup: Mr. Weir is advising on yearbook ad sales strategy
jokes.punchline: Three consecutive reversals of position in under 30 seconds — Mr. Weir doesn't actually know what the right answer is but has an authoritative manner for all of them
jokes.explanation: A great Mr. Weir joke. He embodies the dad who has opinions about everything and confidence in all of them simultaneously. The rapid reversal (split up / teams are better / actually split up) and Cindy's increasingly bewildered 'Thank you, Mr. Weir' is perfectly executed. This is rich character comedy that lands hard because it's immediately recognizable as a real type of person.

jokes.text: Mr. Weir's final word to the employee: 'Sir, don't open that bait.'
jokes.setup: Sam and Cindy have left; Mr. Weir turns to his employee
jokes.punchline: After all the sales advice and parenting, Mr. Weir's final contribution is a warning about bait — he returns instantly to store operations
jokes.explanation: A small but perfect button joke. After all the big-scene comedy, Mr. Weir closes with 'Sir, don't open that bait' — a mundane store-owner concern that recontextualizes the whole scene. Economy is nearly perfect at five words.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Mrs. Weir respond to Mr. Weir saying 'Sam's not afraid to show off his girlfriend' / 'Mom!' / 'What's eating you?'
jokes.setup: Mr. Weir notes that Sam isn't afraid to show off his girlfriend (Cindy), implying Lindsay should introduce her boyfriend
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's 'Mom!' at Mrs. Weir, who hasn't done anything — misplaced exasperation at the wrong parent
jokes.explanation: Small family-dynamics joke. Lindsay's reflexive 'Mom!' when it's actually Dad doing the mortifying is a realistic teen response. Functional.

jokes.text: Mr. Weir: 'Was he the one that ate all my fruit roll-ups?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay reveals her potential boyfriend is Nick
jokes.punchline: Mr. Weir's response to learning his daughter's boyfriend's name is 'Was he the one who ate all my fruit roll-ups?' — Nick is primarily a snack thief in Harold's worldview
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's two or three best jokes. Mr. Weir's primary concern about his daughter's boyfriend is that he ate his fruit roll-ups — this is simultaneously absurd (who cares about fruit roll-ups?), completely realistic (dads remember petty slights), and devastatingly character-specific. The specificity of 'fruit roll-ups' rather than 'snacks' or 'food' is what makes it a great comedy-writing choice. Earns comparison to top-tier sitcom specificity. The joke does triple duty: reveals Mr. Weir's character, reveals that Nick has been in the house before, and reframes the boyfriend reveal through completely unexpected domestic grievance.

jokes.text: Mr. Weir's parental ultimatum: 'You'd better know right now that your mother and I are not taking care of some wedlock baby while you're off gallivanting at college.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Weir is building to forbidding Lindsay from seeing Nick
jokes.punchline: He leaps from 'you kissed a boy' to 'we will not raise your illegitimate child' in one sentence
jokes.explanation: 'Wedlock baby' and 'gallivanting at college' are both period-specific and character-perfect. The jump from flower to grandchild is a classic anxious-parent escalation played for comedy. 'Gallivanting' is a great word choice.

jokes.text: Mrs. Weir's perfectly timed reaction: 'Well, at least she didn't yell.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Weir has just delivered his dramatic 'I absolutely forbid you from seeing this boy' ultimatum
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Weir finds the silver lining: Lindsay's response wasn't loud
jokes.explanation: Perfect button joke. After Harold's big dramatic speech, Jean finds comfort in the absence of shouting. It tells us everything about how the Weirs operate and is delivered in six words. The comic timing is entirely dependent on placement — it only works here.

jokes.text: Mr. Weir: 'I know what they're like. I was one of them.' / Mrs. Weir: 'Ugh! He was.' / Mr. Weir: 'I know. You find it hard to believe.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Weir warns Lindsay that not all boys respect girls
jokes.punchline: Mrs. Weir's 'Ugh, he was' confirms Harold's degenerate-boy past; Harold is flattered by it
jokes.explanation: A nice marital comedy beat. Jean's 'Ugh, he was' is both a confirmation and a jab, and Harold's smug 'I know, you find it hard to believe' — interpreting her disgust as disbelief — is great character work.

jokes.text: Mr. Weir's Korea story: 'I followed a couple of my buddies to the... red-light district.' / Mrs. Weir: 'Your father's first time wasn't special, and he's always regretted it.' / Mr. Weir: 'It was the worst $5.00 I've ever spent. And I wish I could get that $5.00 back.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Weir is trying to warn Lindsay about reckless teenage behavior with a personal cautionary tale
jokes.punchline: The cautionary tale turns out to be that he visited a Korean prostitute — and Mrs. Weir matter-of-factly explains it to Lindsay — and Harold's deepest regret is the five dollars
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best extended joke sequence. The structure is masterful: the ominous buildup ('one night...'), the progressive revelation ('red-light district'), Mrs. Weir's clinical translation for Lindsay, and then the punchline — 'It was the worst $5.00 I've ever spent. And I wish I could get that $5.00 back.' The $5 line is what elevates this to near-legendary status. Harold's authentic regret is about the economic transaction rather than the moral one, and he's completely unaware of the gap. Mrs. Weir facilitating the confession and framing it as a virginity lesson is perfectly absurd. This sequence is directly comparable to the 'Snip, snap' anchor — it's a different type of joke but the level of character integration, escalation, and quotability is comparable. The $5 regret line rivals top sitcom one-liners for economy and character specificity.

jokes.text: Sam's outburst at Bill: 'You know what, Bill? Why don't you just go out with her? Why don't you marry her? I don't care anymore, OK? I don't care. God!'
jokes.setup: Bill is about to say something nice about Cindy; Sam cuts him off with pre-emptive rage
jokes.punchline: Sam's nuclear jealous meltdown ('WHY DON'T YOU MARRY HER') followed immediately by Bill saying Cindy thinks Sam is the nicest guy in school
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection structure: Sam's overreaction is rewarded immediately with the information he actually wanted. The contrast between his 'I DON'T CARE' meltdown and Bill's calm 'she thinks you're the nicest guy in school' is perfect comic timing. Sam's immediate reversal to 'Cindy said THAT?' is the button.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Bill's been in love with Melissa Karpinski ever since he stood behind her at the Dairy Queen. She was wearing this halter top.'
jokes.setup: Sam confronts Bill about liking Cindy; Neal corrects the record
jokes.punchline: Bill's true love is Melissa Karpinski, identified and memorialized from a Dairy Queen halter-top sighting — a deeply specific and perfectly teenage romantic origin story
jokes.explanation: The Dairy Queen halter-top as the inciting romantic event is a perfect teenage-boy detail. The specificity (Dairy Queen, halter top, stood behind her) is what makes it funny rather than just descriptive. Bill's immediate 'She is one foxy lady' is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Bill, dreamily: 'She is one foxy lady. She doesn't cut the cheese.'
jokes.setup: Neal describes Bill's Dairy Queen crush
jokes.punchline: Bill's ideal woman is defined by two traits: foxiness and no flatulence — the cheese-cutting becomes his primary relationship criterion
jokes.explanation: Exceptional callback. 'She doesn't cut the cheese' as the primary romantic credential — following the entire cheese-cutting forensics debate — is a perfect comedic payoff. The line works on multiple levels: it's absurdist (who defines a crush this way?), character-perfect (Bill is earnest about everything), and rewards the earlier fart subplot. Economy is near-perfect at six words. This is one of the episode's top three jokes and rivals the anchor-level examples in structure and quotability.

jokes.text: The boys' Airplane reference: Sam: 'Surely you will.' / Neal: 'OK.' / Sam: 'Don't call me Shirley.' / Neal: 'BILLY, HAVE YOU EVER SEEN A GROWN MAN NAKED?'
jokes.setup: The boys are making plans to see Airplane again
jokes.punchline: They spontaneously perform bits from Airplane at each other — the Shirley/surely gag, then the grown-man-naked line directed at Bill — because they've seen it multiple times and have the whole thing memorized
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is partly the Airplane bits themselves and partly the accuracy of the depiction — these are exactly the kids who quote movies until the quotes lose all context. 'Billy, have you ever seen a grown man naked?' directed at Bill is both a movie quote and a genuine joke at Bill's expense. The show-within-the-show structure adds a layer.

jokes.text: Sam: 'But... he smells.' [re: inviting Gordon] then: 'Maybe he doesn't even know he smells.' [pause] Neal: 'Are you gonna tell him?'
jokes.setup: Sam suggests inviting Gordon to the movies despite his smell
jokes.punchline: Neal's 'Are you gonna tell him?' — a question that hangs in the air, because obviously nobody wants to tell Gordon he smells
jokes.explanation: Neal's question is perfectly positioned — the pause before it and after it does the work. Five words that sum up the entire social dilemma. The timing is everything.

jokes.text: Sam: 'What kind of deodorant do you use?' / Gordon: 'I know I smell, Sam.'
jokes.setup: Sam is trying to subtly bring up Gordon's smell problem by asking about deodorant
jokes.punchline: Gordon cuts straight through the subtext with complete calm: 'I know I smell, Sam.'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character introduction moment. Sam's terrible segue ('what kind of deodorant do you use?') and Gordon's serene, direct 'I know I smell, Sam' is one of the episode's best jokes. Gordon has complete self-knowledge and self-acceptance — he's the most socially sophisticated kid in the scene despite being the smelly one. The 'Sam' address makes it personal and slightly devastating. Economy is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: Gordon explains his condition: 'It's a medical condition. It's called tryptophanuria. It's genetic.' / Sam: 'Wow! Is there a cure?' / Gordon: 'Not yet, but research is progressing.'
jokes.setup: Gordon has revealed he knows he smells
jokes.punchline: Gordon's clinical explanation (tryptophanuria, genetic) delivered with the affect of a medical professional, followed by Sam's genuine 'Is there a cure?' and Gordon's measured 'research is progressing'
jokes.explanation: Gordon's medical briefing on his own smell condition is a genuine comedic discovery. The joke is that Gordon has processed, categorized, and made peace with his condition to the point where he can give it a Latin name and provide a research update. Sam's sincere 'Is there a cure?' is the perfect straight-man response. 'Not yet, but research is progressing' is an exceptional line — Gordon speaks as though he is following the clinical literature. This entire exchange is the episode's most surprising comedy stretch.

jokes.text: Gordon: 'So you're going to be...smelly the rest of your life?' / Gordon: 'Mm-hmm.' [pause] 'Nice people don't care, and it weeds out the jerks. My mom says it's a gift.'
jokes.setup: Sam processes that Gordon's condition is permanent
jokes.punchline: Gordon's philosophical reframe — the smell is a social filter — capped by 'My mom says it's a gift'
jokes.explanation: The philosophical pivot — 'Nice people don't care, and it weeds out the jerks' — is the thematic heart of the episode and also genuinely funny. Gordon's mother calling it a gift is both a loving-parent joke and a real emotional moment. The joke does what great sitcom comedy should: it's funny AND it means something. 'My mom says it's a gift' is one of the episode's most quotable and resonant lines. Earns its high craft score — the originality and character integration are exceptional.

jokes.text: Gordon: 'I'll try not to stink up the joint.' [beat] 'Just kidding.'
jokes.setup: Sam invites Gordon to the movies
jokes.punchline: Gordon makes a self-deprecating smell joke and immediately tags it with 'Just kidding' — showing he has a self-aware sense of humor about his condition
jokes.explanation: Gordon's self-deprecating joke is earned — he's established his self-acceptance — and the 'just kidding' is a perfect beat. The exchange between Sam's nervous laugh and Gordon's clarification is warm and funny.

jokes.text: Bill mockingly imitates Cindy's voice: '[imitates Cindy] See you after school.' — right after she leaves
jokes.setup: Cindy has just invited Sam to come to Sackie's after school and walked away
jokes.punchline: Bill immediately does a mocking impression of Cindy — establishing that he finds Sam's crush ridiculous — right in Sam's face
jokes.explanation: Bill's immediate mockery is in-character and quick. The timing — the instant she's gone — is what makes it work. Standard best-friend ribbing executed well.

jokes.text: Sam says he expected Cindy to like healthy food / Cindy: 'If I never saw another carrot again, it'd be too soon. I mean, my parents never take us to Sackie's. That's why I have to sneak here after school.'
jokes.setup: Sam assumed cheerleader Cindy eats healthy food like carrots
jokes.punchline: Cindy's passionate anti-carrot stance and revelation that she sneaks to the burger joint — she's constrained by health-conscious parents, not genuinely healthy
jokes.explanation: The callback to Cindy's carrot stick life at home — she has them because her parents force it, not by choice — is a good subversion of the 'perfect cheerleader' image. The episode uses Cindy to show the gap between public persona and private reality, and this is a funny way to do it.

jokes.text: Cindy confesses her crush on Todd Schellinger and then says to Sam: 'It's not like with you. You're so easy to talk to. You're just like my sister.'
jokes.setup: Sam is on what he believes is a quasi-date with Cindy; she's confiding in him about another boy
jokes.punchline: 'You're just like my sister' — the nuclear friendzone delivered with complete obliviousness by Cindy
jokes.explanation: A devastating moment that is also extremely funny because of how completely Cindy doesn't register what she's said. 'You're just like my sister' is a specifically terrible variant of the friendzone — not even compared to a male friend, but a sister. Sam's 'Thanks' in response, and then his immediate devastated silence, is the punchline. The episode has been building Sam's hope perfectly and this is the collapse. In terms of cringe comedy, this is near-perfect structure.

jokes.text: Cindy immediately after: 'My God, did he just look at me? Don't look.' — about Todd Schellinger in the same restaurant
jokes.setup: Cindy has just told Sam he's like her sister; she immediately pivots to Todd
jokes.punchline: Zero pause before Cindy's attention shifts entirely to Todd — Sam is functionally invisible seconds after being her 'sister'
jokes.explanation: The escalation on Sam's misfortune: not only is he her 'sister,' he's immediately pressed into service as a wingman. Cindy's 'Don't look' implies Sam must now perform invisibility on her behalf. The cruelty is inadvertent, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Sam comes to sit with his parents; opens with 'Why? What did I do?' and his mom laughs 'What did I do?' back at his dad
jokes.setup: Mr. Weir calls Sam over to sit with the parents
jokes.punchline: Sam's instant guilty reflex — 'What did I do?' — when asked to simply join his parents
jokes.explanation: Sam's guilty conscience firing when he's done nothing wrong is a universal teen experience. The parents finding it charming ('what did I do?') is a warm family comedy beat. Brief but effective.

jokes.text: Nick, about the Moody Blues: 'They're kind of wimpy, but I think this song's OK.'
jokes.setup: Nick has put on the Moody Blues to set a romantic mood
jokes.punchline: Nick immediately undercuts his own romantic gesture by noting the Moody Blues are 'kind of wimpy' — his cool-guy self-awareness conflicting with his sentimentality
jokes.explanation: Nick's romantic gestures always contain this self-undermining quality — he's trying to be smooth but can't stop qualifying. 'They're kind of wimpy, but this song's OK' while it's playing in his seduction attempt is a nice character beat.

jokes.text: Lindsay asks why Nick quit basketball. Nick: 'They found a dime bag in my locker, and then they kicked me off the team. And then I just... lost interest.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is asking about his basketball trophies; it seems like a sad story incoming
jokes.punchline: The 'and then I just lost interest' addendum to being kicked off for drugs — as if the expulsion and the loss of interest are two separate sequential events, not causally related
jokes.explanation: The deadpan structure is excellent. 'They found a dime bag in my locker and kicked me off the team. And then I just lost interest.' The second clause is the joke — he treats the drug expulsion and the loss of interest as parallel developments rather than cause-and-effect. Nick's blameless self-narrative is perfectly characterized.

jokes.text: Nick showing Lindsay his basketball trophies: 'They found a dime bag in my locker, and then they kicked me off the team. And then I just lost interest.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay notices Nick's basketball trophies and asks why he quit
jokes.punchline: The dime bag revelation delivered as a casual non-event, followed by 'I just lost interest' — as though the drug possession was a minor contextual detail
jokes.explanation: Nick's complete nonchalance about the dime bag — leading with 'they kicked me off the team' as though that's the remarkable part — is perfect Nick characterization. 'And then I just lost interest' is the key line, suggesting that without the team, the sport itself held no appeal. Delivered as if this is a totally normal sporting career arc.

jokes.text: Nick has set up candles, a record player, and a carefully selected song. He says: 'Lindsay... this song says all the things that I haven't been able to say to you. It's a little corny. But I mean it.' Then sings along with the record.
jokes.setup: Nick has orchestrated an elaborate romantic scene
jokes.punchline: Nick singing along with a Barry Manilow-style love ballad, earnestly, while making meaningful eye contact with Lindsay — the cringe is total
jokes.explanation: Extended cringe sequence. Nick acknowledges 'it's a little corny' — self-awareness that doesn't prevent him from doing it anyway — and then sings along, adding his own commentary between verses. The gap between Nick's sincere romantic intent and the ambient absurdity of being serenaded by a stoner in his basement is the joke. Lindsay's expression during this sequence (implied) carries as much as Nick's performance.

jokes.text: Nick, mid-song, breaks from lyrics to say: 'See, Lindsay, we were made for each other. You want to make out or something?'
jokes.setup: Nick has been building an elaborate romantic speech about how they're not rushing anything and have all the time in the world
jokes.punchline: 'You want to make out or something?' — delivered conversationally, completely undermining his own 'we're not rushing' thesis
jokes.explanation: The episode's single most perfectly constructed joke. Nick has delivered an extended speech about how their connection can't be rushed, capped with 'What's better than this?' — and then immediately asks to make out. The structure is flawless: the setup is long, the subversion is instant, and the word 'something' ('make out or something') is the genius touch — casual, hopeful, like he's suggesting they grab a sandwich. Only Nick could deliver this. The character integration is total; no one else in the show could have earned this line. It lands on the level of the anchor examples — it's different in register (dumb-sweet vs. sharp-witty) but mechanically equivalent in perfection.

jokes.text: Nick, after Lindsay says no to making out: 'All guys want to make out. But I just want to hold you.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay declines the make-out offer
jokes.punchline: Nick's pivot to 'I just want to hold you' — either genuine or an immediate recovery — delivered while presumably making intense eye contact
jokes.explanation: The callback squeeze on joke 81 — Nick pivots to the sincere high road instantly, which could be manipulation or genuine sweetness. The show keeps Nick's intentions intentionally ambiguous, which is the right call. 'All guys want to make out' as a universal truth Nick is exempting himself from is slightly absurdist.

jokes.text: Nick: 'What's better than this?' / Lindsay reaches over and takes his Ding Dong without asking. Nick: 'Hey. Can I have the Ding Dong?' / Lindsay: 'Sure.'
jokes.setup: Nick has just delivered his most sincere romantic statement; they're holding each other in the candlelit basement
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately takes his snack; his response is 'Can I have the Ding Dong?' — she already has it
jokes.explanation: Perfect scene-closing joke. After all of Nick's romantic apparatus, the scene ends with Lindsay stealing his Ding Dong and him politely asking for it back. The bathos is total — from 'what's better than this' to 'can I have the Ding Dong' in thirty seconds. The word 'Ding Dong' is doing work that a more sophisticated snack name could not.

jokes.text: Sam comes home and Lindsay immediately asks: 'How was your date?' / Sam: 'It was OK. Mom and Dad made me watch TV with them all night.' / Lindsay: 'They were weird.' / Sam: 'Yeah, what else is new?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay had a romantic evening; Sam's 'date' with Cindy turned into forced family TV time
jokes.punchline: Sam's 'date' was watching television with his parents — the absolute opposite of what he planned
jokes.explanation: Good payoff on Sam's storyline. The contrast between Nick's elaborate romantic basement setup and Sam's evening watching TV with mom and dad is cleanly funny. 'Yeah, what else is new?' is nice sibling-shorthand.

jokes.text: Sam: 'I don't need another friend. I already have 2. I mean, how many more friends does a guy need?'
jokes.setup: Sam is processing that Cindy only likes him as a friend; he's despondent
jokes.punchline: His self-pity lands on the logic that he can't take another friend because he already has exactly two — the small, precise number (not 'a few,' not 'some') is the joke
jokes.explanation: An excellent joke. 'I already have 2' is the line — the specificity of 'two' as Sam's friend count, stated without embarrassment as a real number, is perfectly character-integrated. It's both funny and genuinely poignant. The follow-up 'How many more friends does a guy need?' as a rhetorical question about two friends is the cap. Could only be Sam; could only be this show. Justifies 8.0.

jokes.text: Lindsay's phone call from Cindy — Lindsay answering excitedly, clearly being asked to be a conduit to Sam, saying 'Todd called?'... 'Yeah, it's great'... 'He said that?'... until she commits to listening to Cindy's Todd drama indefinitely
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just discovered Nick as her boyfriend; things seem resolved romantically
jokes.punchline: Cindy calls — but not about Sam, about Todd — and Lindsay is now trapped receiving second-hand teen romance updates for a crush she was just listening to in person
jokes.explanation: A good structural callback to close the episode. After Sam is friendzoned, Cindy pivots the same energy to Lindsay — pulling her in as another confidante for the Todd saga. 'Tell me what else happened' as the final line — Lindsay fully invested despite herself — is a nice note to close on. Thematic resonance: everyone is trapped listening to someone else's romantic problems.

tmdb_overview: Sam tries out to be the school mascot hoping to impress Cindy. Meanwhile, Daniel, Ken, and Kim suddenly become the basketball team's most rabid fans following a series of run-ins with jocks from the rival school, Lincoln High. Lindsay tries to figure out the best way to break up with Nick.
